data.title: DOJ Vs. Powell: We Have A Valuation Problem (And 2 Strategies For Now)
data.description:
The FED And Its Independence

The FED seems under attack, and this time Jerome Powell wanted to clearly state that the DOJ is investigating him as "a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President". At stake is the FED's independence.

This is where the escalation between the FED and the government seems to have reached a point of no return, creating a particular environment that can be discussed in many ways. In this article, we will focus on the implications for the market and us as investors, avoiding any political angle (there are appropriate places on Seeking Alpha to discuss politics).

This is particularly interesting because the U.S. was born by demanding independence, and the idea of "checks and balances" is deeply rooted in its institutions.

The FED was created in 1913, after the 1907 bank run. It was independent but still closely linked to the Treasury. For example, during the Great Depression, President Roosevelt and Congress mandated that the FED issue reserve notes that were not backed by gold, even though, at that time, the gold standard was effective. Eventually, the Fed, too, won its independence after WWII in a famous clash against President Truman. During the war, the Fed kept interest rates at around three-eighths on Treasuries. Once the war was over, the FED wanted to go back to a normal interest rate policy, which was particularly needed as inflation was high (over 20% in 1951). President Truman wanted low rates, but the FED didn't agree, and this went on until the "Treasury-Fed Accord of 1951" was signed, which began the era of the FED's independence in deciding monetary policy without having to consider debt management policies. Finally, in 1977, Congress approved the Federal Reserve Reform Act, which explicitly gave the FED the dual maximum employment/stable prices mandate that it still has today.

There have been other times when the Fed's chair and the President clashed (Johnson vs. Martin in 1965; Nixon vs. Burns in the early 70s; Bush Sr. vs. Greenspan in 1991-1992). Sometimes, the Fed has been the scapegoat that presidents used to blame for a cooling economy. But today's clash seems similar to what happened with Truman, and the outcome could even be a regime shift. While it is too early to speculate on that, I would like to focus on the value of the FED's independence, not from a moral or political point of view, but from the standpoint of investors. In other words, do investors pay a premium for this independence? Do they value it?

The Value Of The FED's Independence

Now, there is no FED ticker. So, what we are going to do is to understand how the FED's independence is embedded in key prices that make sense only if we assume that the FED is free to follow the monetary policy it wants without any interference from outside.

When we look at the 5-yr expected inflation, we see that, last month, we were at 2.24%, which is actually quite close to the FED's target of 2%. This is even more striking as inflation is right now stabilizing around 3%, and the 1-year expected inflation is at 3.2%. In other words, it seems as if the markets still believed (before the investigation, of course) that the FED would have obviously stuck to its 2% inflation goal. If we see a sudden spike in inflation expectations before a true reacceleration in the next two or three CPI prints, then we would see that markets are probably fearing a political interference affecting the FED.

Some may say that this is just about expectations and that there is no real threat to our investments. Not really, I would argue. When inflation expectations move up, you see that the nominal yields and required returns are also impacted. Everything is, in a certain way, repriced because investors increase the return rates they demand to protect themselves from being destroyed by high inflation.

The first signal came from today's gold price (let's use the ETF GLD as a proxy), which was up 2.4% in a move that I consider a true reaction to the news. When inflation expectations are influenced by politics, investors seek refuge in gold. In particular, while bonds can tell us what the market expects from the FED and the yield required to lend money to the government, gold simply tells us whether investors feel or not that the FED can truly pursue its goals and deliver accordingly. As the "higher-for-longer" narrative grew, leading many to believe that we won't see 2% for inflation for several years, gold - unsurprisingly - started rallying, fueled also by the numerous geopolitical crises around the world that make it stand out as a safe haven.

Secondly, when expected inflation ticks higher, nominal yields go up as well. This implies that bond prices move down. Usually, if expected long-term inflation moves up by 1 percentage point, we can see a drop in long-duration bond prices between 8% and 15%. This is simply how the math of repricing works.

Third. If inflation expectations move up, the risk-free rate also does. The equity premium also goes up because investors price more political uncertainty. The outcome is that investors use higher discount rates, which is bearish for valuations. Remember what happened in 2022, when investors realized that inflation was too hot? We had a bear market (by the way, it was a great moment to buy).

Fourth. Admitting that the FED protects its independence, when inflation expectations trend higher, the FED is usually forced to extend its restrictive policy, fueling the higher-for-longer scenario.

Who pays? Bonds and unprofitable companies whose profits are out in the future. Who wins? Not many. Gold (and safe havens). Perhaps short-duration credits. But even businesses with pricing power and superb quality could be brought down by a renewed bear market.

This explains why inflation expectations are extremely valuable for investors. They should never be overlooked.

We are accustomed to seeing analysts use the 10-year Treasury yield as the risk-free rate. However, an earthquake took place in May 2025 when Moody's downgraded the U.S. debt. This is when the valuation authority professor, Aswath Damodaran, explained that we could not think of U.S. Treasuries as completely risk-free. It was not a matter of political or philosophical beliefs; it was a matter of looking at how the market was reacting and starting to reprice the equity risk premium for U.S. stocks, although by a little.

Strategic Implications

1. Gold: The Anti-Political Asset

Let's go back to gold. In a normal environment, Gold usually trades inversely to real interest rates, meaning that when inflation is high, gold moves up, and when inflation is low, gold usually decelerates. But as we move on, we are learning (or we are simply rediscovering) that gold can be priced as an asset that has no counterparty. Let me explain what this means. If the U.S. government charges the Fed chair and threatens him with jail, then risk-free assets, such as U.S. Treasuries, start having a political connotation. They carry a political weight. This is because Treasuries have a counterparty. On the other hand, gold has none, and investors like this. It also seems that central banks around the world, the so-called whales, believe gold is better than Treasuries right now, and this is why we are seeing countries such as China or Poland buying gold hand over fist. Clearly, in this attempt to diversify and reduce their exposure to the U.S. dollar, they are pushing gold prices to the moon. But the clash between the FED and the DOJ will only bring additional fuel to this belief, incentivizing further divestiture from the U.S. currency.

Now, while we saw gold catching investors' eyes as inflation was too high after the pandemic, we also have to realize that the reason why investors kept holding gold has developed and changed over time. Right now, it is "politically risk-free", and this seems to matter more compared to what inflation will do from now on. Basically, when we are buying gold, we are now buying a premium caused by distrust in political institutions.

In this environment, holding gold could be a bet on further institutional disruption. As far as I am concerned, I won't be part of this move. I see it as a real bet, instead of an investment decision, because it relies on too many unknowns. Furthermore, as much as I like buying gold rings and jewels for my wife (besides their beauty, I like that they usually don't go down in value), I usually want to invest in real cash-producing assets. In the long run, I am a firm believer that the power of labor and innovation is a better way to grow the value of my investments rather than relying on commodity price fluctuations. Gold is in a league of its own, but there are plenty of tables showing that over long periods of time, stocks are the best way to go. Some may say that gold miners could be a better choice because they lagged the metal, and they will have to eventually catch up. It could be a reasonable take, because if political risk keeps being high or even rises more, then prices will remain extremely high and will eventually show in many companies' earnings. But, if you have followed my reasoning, investors are looking for hedges. Miners are not. Therefore, I doubt that they will attract big inflows of money as gold itself is doing right now.

2. Short-Treasuries

If the Fed is going to lose its independence (it won't take just one investigation from the DOJ to achieve this goal, but what happened does push in this direction), then, as we saw above, expectations around inflation could soar. This would imply seeing 10-yr Treasuries tank as their yields move up. Long-term bonds are simply to be avoided. This is why short-term Treasuries could become more interesting (SHV). Right now, they yield around 4.2%, and because of their maturity date, they have low sensitivity to a change in rates. We can see it clearly below. Once the Fed finished increasing interest rates, SHV has simply been trading within a well-defined range. This means that short-term Treasuries are almost completely immune to duration risk. We know that the clash between the President and the Fed chair is because of interest rates, with the former wanting a dovish policy. Right now, trying to guess where long-term rates will fall is extremely dangerous because we don't know how things will develop.

Let's assume that the Fed will see its independence somewhat weakened. This means that it will probably cut rates more than it would like to. As a result, long-term rates will probably rise because they will factor in a renewed inflation risk. The yield curve will therefore steepen, and this is bad for long-term bonds because it pushes their prices down. Holding short-term Treasuries is a way to earn a decent yield on cash while giving us the opportunity to avoid guessing correctly what the curve will do. SHV is what some could call a preserver of optionality. It keeps our capital intact and allows us to have flexibility when it comes to timing our next investments.

As some may know, I like to always have some cash aside. Until now, I had some accounts where my cash was earning 5%. But now the yield has moved down, and SHV is the likeliest candidate I am considering to park the liquidity I hold that I want to eventually deploy in the market when opportunities arise. SHV is not truly an investment; it's not about having a bullish or bearish outlook on the market (by the way, I am bullish), but it prevents us from paying a high price if we make the wrong bet on where rates will fall.

Conclusion

We are really in the early stages, even though this is the peak of an ongoing institutional clash (or friction). However, we are now at a point where the financial system is going through a stress test. The market has been used to seeing a well-defined barrier between monetary and fiscal policies. If this is blown away, the market will take its toll. Currently, we are seeing some signs of an increasing political risk priced into U.S. equities. Risk-free is changing its meaning, and it is becoming more and more linked with gold rather than U.S. Treasuries. Gold is there to prove it to us. In addition, I would never consider cash as trash right now. It is an investment in flexibility that earns us a very decent return while allowing us to make the best investing decisions. While I am a big fan of seeking return on capital, sometimes I believe that being sure that, first and foremost, the return of capital is 100% guaranteed is a short-term scenario that we have to endure, whether we like it or not.

I am not saying that we should be bearish on the market. As I have often explained, I am actually bullish and believe 2026 will reward some clear winners while also paving the way for earnings growth breadth across many sectors. For those who are a bit scared, I could also suggest a much more relaxing and calming piece where I explained that there are at least 5 things that won't change in 2026, offering us very compelling investing opportunities without having to worry every day about headlines. But, surely, in the light of this new development, I would consider keeping some dry powder aside to be able to make the most out of sudden opportunities that open up.

data.title: SMDV: Why I Still Like It
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome The Intelligent ETF Investor as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

I started writing about exchange-traded funds (ETFs) at Seeking Alpha last year, but I have been following the space for a very long time. I am a big believer in ETFs! They appeal to individual investors and also to many investment managers and advisors. Low costs, high liquidity, and the idea of buying an index make sense, though there are many ETFs that fall outside the passive index mode.

I wrote about ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (SMDV) in early November, and it has rallied. My initial view was bullish, and I still like it and am updating my view on SMDV today.

The SMDV Price Action

Since the initial piece I wrote in early November was published, SMDV has gone up. The ETF had a rough 2025. Here is the three-year chart:

In 2025, the ETF had a total return of 0.3%, with the dividends overcoming the 2.4% price decline. The stock is still below its late 2023 peak and well above the low set in late 2024. The ETF had gapped up in late November, but a pullback during trading on 12/31 filled that gap.

As I discussed in that last piece, the ETF, which is focused on small caps with good dividend growth, has performed poorly relative to large-caps. Here is the longer-term perspective of SMDV and the Russell 2000 (IWM) and the S&P 500 (SPY):

Over the past decade, SMDV has lagged SPY, but it has also lagged IWM. This lag is fully explained by the market action since stocks were hit hard in early 2020 following the pandemic arriving in America.

I created an ETF Focus List that currently has 78 ETFs, several of which are dividend-related. I have written about some of these at Seeking Alpha and had Sell ratings on Schwab U.S. Dividend ETF (SCHD) and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF Shares (VIG). Here is how SMDV has performed against several other dividend ETFs over the past three years (since 12/31/22):

Of these dividend-focused ETFs, SMDV has outpaced only one, Invesco High Yield Dividend Achievers ETF (PEY) and has lagged the other five. The total return is less than half that of VIG. SMDV has an expense ratio of 0.4%, which seems reasonable to me though a lot higher than many of these other ETFs.

SMDV Composition

In the last piece, I discussed several factors about SMDV, including its year of inception (2015) and the manager, ProShares, which runs 50 ETFs, including eight that are dividend-focused. SMDV is the third-largest and S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (NOBL) is the largest. I wrote about this one recently, rating it Hold, though I do include it in my ETF model portfolio and my wife did add it this week.

SMDV is the only ETF focused on the dividend growers in the Russell 2000. Most of the dividend-focused ETFs and funds are focused on large-caps. The ProShares website for SMDV shares the holdings, which include 104 stocks with a current assets under management of $637 million. Here is the look at the top names:

I like that the fund has equal weightings of its members when it rebalances. Here is the ETF by sector:

The ETF has a lot of exposure to Financials, Industrials and Utilities, and I like that it has very little exposure to Information Technology. Here are some characteristics of SMDV and the other six dividend-focused ETFs that I had in the chart above as well as SPY:

SMDV is focused on small-caps, and it is small. It is also not widely followed. The contrarian in me digs this!

Why I Still Like SMDV

While small-caps and SMDV have rallied recently, I still like the ETF at this higher price. I like small-caps. That doesn't mean that I will always like that part of the market or every single small-cap, but small-caps have performed poorly relative to large-caps for a while now. This has happened as some large-caps have done exceptionally well. The S&P 500 has gotten very concentrated in just a few names and has picked up a large amount of exposure to the Technology sector. I like that SMDV is well diversified and has little exposure to the large S&P 500 names.

As I mentioned when I initiated coverage of SMDV, the M&A environment has gotten better, and this could be good for the small-caps in the ETF.

I understand why people like dividend ETFs, and they have done very well compared to this one. The Sell ratings that I issued for VIG and for SCHD in October suggested cash or TIPS as alternatives, but that doesn't do it for many investors. I think that SMDV is an excellent replacement for these and other dividend ETFs that are large-cap focused.

Conclusion

I like SMDV despite its recent rally. I created a model portfolio for ETFs at year-end, and SMDV was one of the six ETFs included (at a weight of 15%). I realize that the ETF has jumped since I first called it out and in 2026 so far, but it looks cheap to me. I don't see any chart challenges, with the stock still well below the late high in 2024. The focus on small-caps, the higher cost than other funds, and the small size scare off some, I suppose, but this bearish analyst thinks it can hold in better if stocks weaken or perhaps rally a lot if they don't.

data.title: American Express Downgraded As Trump Floats Credit Card Rate Cap
data.description:
President Trump attacked not only Fed Chair Powell in recent months, but over the weekend, he took verbal aim at credit card companies. The POTUS floated the idea of capping credit card interest rates at 10%, which, if made law, would significantly hurt earnings for the likes of American Express (AXP), Visa (V), and Mastercard (MA).

Indeed, shares of those companies cratered on Monday, Jan. 12, with AXP being hurt the most. Today, ahead of its January 30 Q4 earnings report, I am downgrading AXP from a "Buy" to a "Hold." I was upbeat on the valuation and technicals of the now $252 billion market cap Financials sector company back in November 2023.

Shares are up a whopping 155% since then, more than doubling the S&P 500’s return. But the valuation is now stretched, while the technicals are not wholly bullish.

AXP Outperforming XLF and SPY YoY

Back in October, AXP reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $4.14 beat the Wall Street consensus forecast of $3.98, while revenue of $18.4 billion, up 11% from the same period a year ago, was a material $380 million beat. The management team hiked its full-year 2025 revenue guidance from 9% to 10%, while seeing operating EPS in the range of $15.20-$15.50. Provisions for credit losses fell 5% YoY.

Shares soared by 7.3% in the session that followed—the best earnings reaction going back to January 2023, breaking a string of five consecutive moves lower post-reporting. Looking ahead to the Jan. 30 Q4 release, the options market prices in a somewhat elevated 4.5% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the reporting date. Implied volatility is back above 30%, while short interest on the stock is low at 1.42%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, AXP delivered strong Q3 results, driven by high card usage trends and despite higher operating expenses. Its Global Business Travel Group merger contributed modestly to the bottom line, while upbeat CEO commentary was something the bulls pointed to. Specifically, executives highlighted strong early momentum from the Platinum Card refresh (with its higher fees being most accretive to earnings in Q4 2026), including doubled account acquisitions and higher engagement. Billings accelerated broadly, especially in travel and entertainment, and share buybacks rose to $2.3 billion in the third quarter—a key part of the bulls’ thesis.

Solid consumer spending, particularly in travel and leisure, along with corporate trends, supported sales growth over the July through September period. Those trends appeared to continue through year-end, too, with early holiday spending up 9% YoY as of early December. We’ll get the full Q4 details at the end of this month.

On the call, the company reaffirmed its commitment to mid-teens EPS growth, even with short-term margin pressures from its investments. I’ll be on the lookout for details on how any proposed credit card interest rate cap may impact the company, which is more linked to higher-end consumers and the corporate space, versus the lower half of US consumers.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be strong in CY 2026 and through the out year. A solid low- to mid-teens bottom-line growth rate supports a market multiple, but I’ll note later that the P/E has gotten a bit stretched. Still, the sellside has turned much more upbeat, with 20 upgrades compared to just six downgrades.

Finally, American Express is among the most profitable Financials sector companies, now with free cash flow per share of $27.14 over the past 12 months.

American Express: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates to my 2023 analysis. If we assume $18 of operating EPS over the next 12 months and apply a 21x P/E (above its 5-year average), then shares should trade near $378.

The earnings multiple is in line with the market’s, which I assert is about right, given its historical P/E trend both on an absolute basis and relative to the SPX. Shares also trade a bit rich on a price-to-sales basis.

AXP: Shares Have Become Fully Priced

Key risks for AXP include weaker spending trends among higher-wealth households, perhaps triggered by weaker equity markets this year. Moreover, a global macro slowdown could crimp enterprise and corporate travel spending—a key growth area for the company. Of course, if a 10% credit card interest rate cap is inked into law, that would prompt a significant operational change, and likely a higher risk premium.

For AXP specifically, we’ll see how the Platinum refresh is trending within the Q4 report and on the earnings call. Also, up-and-coming fintech players, including BNPL and crypto banking names, could represent a long-term competitive threat.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Friday, Jan. 30, BMO. Shares pay a $0.82 quarterly dividend on Tuesday, Feb. 10, the same day that the management team presents at the UBS Financial Services Conference 2026.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares now close to what I consider to be fair value, AXP’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that while a clear series of higher highs and higher lows is in place, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph has printed lower highs. That's non-confirmation between momentum and price. I would not be surprised if AXP drifted lower into the Q4 report, eventually testing the rising long-term 200-day moving average later this year.

In the here and now, an uptrend support line is in play, currently near the $355 mark, while horizontal support is seen between $326 and $329. It’s important not to lose sight of the broader trend—up. Thus, it’s a "buy the dip" play if we do see a correction into the low-mid $300s. That would also mark a more favorable valuation long play. Big picture, a measured move upside price target to about $430 is in play based on the depth of the January to April decline and subsequent breakout.

AXP: Uptrend In Place, But Weaker RSI, Long-Term $430 Technical Target

The Bottom Line

I have a "Hold" rating on AXP. I see the credit card company’s shares as now close to fair value, while the chart sends mixed signals heading into the Q4 earnings report.

data.title: Critical Metals: Greenland Headlines And A Potential U.S. Stake Could Rerate It
data.description:
Critical Metals Corp. (CRML) is on a rollercoaster ride. Since rumors surfaced that the U.S. would take an equity stake in the company, shares tripled into the October highs, then sold off by 80% in the weeks after.

This year, the stock doubled after the U.S. interest in gaining control over Greenland showed no signs of slowing down.

Just like with MP Materials (MP) last year, fundamentals have little to do with the recent price action. Sentiment (mainly driven by headlines) is what's moving shares.

Overall, I see a few catalysts this year that could reignite shareholder enthusiasm (and therefore, a rally). First, the pilot plant in Qaqortoq is likely to be operational before the end of May. Second, the 300–500 kg/hour POC pilot plant in Romania is scheduled for Q2 this year. I admit that the upside from a fundamentals perspective will likely be limited this year. All I see is a possible improvement if the US takes a stake in the company. This could be the third catalyst to move shares, given the high capital needed to advance the Tanbrezz project in Greenland.

Finally, and this is a nice fat tail event, if the U.S. manages to strike a deal with Greenland (I don't believe annexation is likely), and if the terms include clauses for REEs, I believe CRML could see further upside (again, from a sentiment perspective).

Overall, I see CRML as a trading stock, not a long-term buy-and-hold opportunity. I rate it a cautious Buy, and I own a small position myself.

Business Overview And Recent Developments

CRML is a development-stage critical minerals company whose core value is the Tanbreez rare earth element project in Greenland.

It currently owns 42% (minority stake) of the Tanbreez project, so broadly, the bull case assumes one of the two scenarios:

  1. CRML monetizes its minority stake via future mine cash flows. To achieve this, they will need funding, which I don't think will be a big problem, as I will discuss in a moment.
  2. With time, CRML increases its ownership in the project. Back in September, the company announced intentions to step up from a minority stake to a controlling stake (92.5%) via an amended acquisition agreement with Rimbal, subject to Greenland approval. More on this topic in just a moment.

Earlier drilling results show relatively stable TREO grades at Tanbreez and a 25–27% heavy rare earth oxides share. This is great news, given that heavy REEs are typically scarcer and can be strategically valuable for magnets in defense applications.

The latest investor presentation cites a Tanbreez preliminary economic assessment, with NPV of US$3B, an IRR of 180%, and >US$26B revenue over a 25+ year mine life:

To convert the REE concentrate, the company recently announced a proposed processing route via a 50/50 JV with Romania’s state-owned Fabrica de Prelucrare a Concentratelor de Uraniu S.R.L. Plans include a 300–500 kg/hour POC pilot plant costing approximately $2 million, with commissioning expected in Q2 2026.

In line with this timeframe, the company announced that it has commenced construction of the Tanbreez pilot plant in Qaqortoq, with the pilot plant section targeted for no later than May 2026.

Now, not all concentrate from Tanbreez will be routed to Romania for post-processing. In the PR, CRML stated that only 50% of the Tanbreez concentrate will be sent to the Romania JV. The rest, I believe, will be sent to the US, given the Letter of Intent with Ucore Rare Metals Inc. (TSXV: UCU). For context, last year, CRML signed an LOI with Ucore for a 10-year offtake arrangement, including up to 10,000 metric tons annually of rare earth concentrate (about 10% of the initial projected Tanbreez production).

On top of that, the company secured an additional offtake agreement for 15% of production with REalloys.

Aside from REEs in Greenland, the company has exposure to a lithium project in Austria (Wolfsberg). That said, this project is currently on hold pending price recovery, and there are some regulatory items related to an environmental impact assessment, which add uncertainty to this project.

The Bull Case

First, from a fundamentals perspective, Tanbreez is still years away from revenue. Aside from the pilot plant in Qaqortoq this year (before May 2026), which I believe will bring some revenue in 2026, given the commissioning of the 300–500 kg/hr proof-of-concept pilot plant in Romania, the latest disclosed timeline implies mining starting in 2027 and first production around mid-2028.

As I hinted in the last section, Tanbreez is moving towards pilot production, with a target to have a pilot plant ready by May 2026. Well, it turns out that a high proportion of the planned output at Tanbreez is already secured. Management recently said that 75% of planned output is pre-sold, with the remaining 25% targeted for early 2026.

This is good news until one realizes the funding that is required. According to some sources, $500m in CapEx would be required in Greenland, plus another $1b for downstream processing facilities.

Therefore, the main pillar of the bull case is the company's success in securing this funding.

CRML has not published a newer balance sheet than June 30, 2025. Back then, cash on hand was $7.3m, and the net cash used in operating activities was $14.5 million.

Now, after the October 5 and 15 PIPE transactions, an F-3 form shows (adjusted) cash and cash equivalents of $87.2 million.

As a side note, the adjustment comes "after giving effect to the transactions contemplated by the Purchase Agreements" (i.e., the October 5 and October 15, 2025 PIPEs).

On top of that, the company secured an LOI from EXIM for up to $120 million as a 15-year term loan concept for Tanbreez. Some sources suggest that the project would need to be "well-capitalized with sufficient equity from strategic investors" before the loan would be approved. Which brings me to the next point.

Trump wants the U.S. to own Greenland for strategic reasons. In fact, during his first presidency, he publicly floated the idea of the U.S. buying Greenland. There is no doubt that there is interest in this area, not only from a geopolitical perspective, but also from a minerals perspective.

Back in October, Reuters reported talks to convert a $50M Defense Production Act (DPA) grant into an equity stake of roughly 8%, potentially structured with warrants. Even though $50M doesn't sound like a lot when compared to the $500M required for the Greenland project, such a development would improve the company's ability to secure more funding from other sources (like the $120M loan with EXIM).

On top of that, an investment by the U.S. government would drive up the sentiment around this stock. Just take a look at MP Material's price action after the US announced an equity investment in July last year.

Risks

It is my view that the stock is being bid on geopolitics and strategic headlines.

A clear example of what happens when the headlines are overtaking fundamentals is MP Materials. After the outperformance that I discussed in the last section, the stock sold off 50% between its highs in October and December 31, 2025.

I warn readers that a similar selloff is likely for CRML, especially if the headline cadence slows down.

As you may know, the stock was down close to 80% after the highs in October, following the rumor of the U.S. equity stake in the company.

The company doesn't have any meaningful valuation ratios given its early stage. All I'm able to do is a rough estimate of the cash runway. Using the $87M from the latest F-3 and a cash burn of $21.2M ($15M in net cash used in operating activities and $7M in net cash used in investing activities), I obtain a runway of 49 months (4.1 years). I believe this estimate is not realistic, given the $500M estimated to advance the Tanbreez mine in Greenland. To achieve this, the company needs external funding. A U.S. stake could be favorable (as it will give credibility to other loan providers), but it is unclear if it will eventually materialize.

Conclusion

In my view, two milestones will determine whether the Tanbreez bull case converts from a narrative into an executable build plan.

The project needs roughly $500M in Greenland CapEx plus $1B for downstream facilities, far beyond the current $87.2M pro forma cash.

The company needs to raise capital via the $120M EXIM term-loan and any U.S. participation, including the reported $50M DPA grant conversion discussion. Until then, dilution remains a key risk to monitor, especially after the stock is up over 100% since the start of the year.

The other milestone is related to the delivery of the Qaqortoq pilot plant section by May 2026 and commissioning of the 300–500 kg/hour Romania POC pilot in Q2 2026. Even though I expect minimal revenue contribution this year, I believe it is important to show progress on the execution side. Any delays could hurt sentiment, and I believe sentiment is what's driving the share price right now.

Overall, I'm bullish given that I believe the U.S. will (at least) make a deal with Greenland. I am unsure if its annexation will happen, but an REE deal is likely given the recent interest in this region.

data.title: Madness And Mayhem Continue In January
data.description:
Happy Monday!

Heading into the traditionally challenging second year of the US presidential cycle, global news flow continues to erode consumer and business confidence. In response, the Trump administration is intensifying efforts to boost popular support through handouts and distractions. In the early going this morning, equity futures are down, along with the US dollar.

Among the top headlines yesterday, President Trump directed the US Department of Justice to serve the Federal Reserve with grand jury subpoenas, threatening a criminal indictment over Chairman Jay Powell’s congressional testimony last June regarding the ongoing renovation of the central bank’s headquarters.

In a written and video statement released Sunday evening, Fed Chair Powell took the gloves off, saying:

“The threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the president.”

Meanwhile, layoffs are continuing at a pace as tariffs and weaker demand bite into profit margins. Companies of all stripes are filing for bankruptcy; large corporate bankruptcies are at their highest level in 15 years. See, Bankruptcies are exploding across the economy, hitting small businesses and households. Few industries are immune:

“Rising costs, tighter credit conditions, and ongoing geopolitical volatility continue to exert pressure on households and businesses already facing financial strain,” Amy Quackenboss, the executive director at the American Bankruptcy Institute, said earlier this month.”

The University of Michigan consumer sentiment index, at 53.3 in December, was 23% below the 69.6 average reading at recession lows since 1953 and only weaker at the May 1980 recession low of 51.7.

Despite record capital spending on artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure, the US Index of Leading Economic Indicators (LEI), published by the Conference Board, has been flat or declining for 10 consecutive months and now sits at an 11-year low. The year-over-year LEI has been negative every month since July 2022 (-3.3% currently), a persistence historically seen only during outright recessions. Absent the positive lift from rising equity markets, the deterioration in leading economic signals would be even more pronounced.

More than at any point in modern history, the economy has become dependent on the direction of the stock market. Forecasts are notoriously backward-looking, with consensus targets historically lagging price moves by roughly two months (Piper Sandler data).

Market prices have therefore proven to be a better leading indicator of forecasts than forecasts are of prices, making it unsurprising that after three consecutive years of double-digit equity gains, Wall Street forecasters are reporting record optimism for 2026.

Historically, the second year of the US presidential cycle (2026) has been the weakest for equities. Since 1948, the S&P 500 has suffered an average peak-to-trough decline of roughly 17% in Year Two and closer to 20% in first-term administrations. The pattern highlights that midterm years often coincide with political uncertainty and economic adjustment, conditions that tend to challenge risk assets even within longer-term bull markets.

In the rare instances when stock prices entered the second year of the presidential cycle at high valuations, in 1962, 1966, 1974, 1990, 2002, 2010, 2018, 2022, drawdowns ranged from -20 to -48%.

The current 23x S&P 500 12-month forward price-to-earnings ratio has only been seen twice before, at bubble peaks in 2000 and 2022; prices fell 48% and 25%, respectively.

The S&P 500’s cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings ratio (CAPE)—price divided by the average inflation-adjusted earnings of the past ten years—now stands near 40×. There has never been an instance in which markets trading above a 35× CAPE did not generate losses over the subsequent 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods.

At the same time, the S&P 500 is trading at roughly 3.33× sales—the highest level on record and about double its long-term median of 1.67×.

The most reliable way to make money in the stock market is not by buying shares, but by creating a company and selling shares to the market—especially when valuations are at record highs. In that case, the outcome is favourable for the seller and far less so for the buyer.

In a recent interview, Elon Musk, the world’s richest person, underscored this reality when asked what companies people should invest in, noting: “I don’t really buy stocks… I don’t have a portfolio or anything. I don’t look for things to invest in. I try to build things, and then there happens to be stock of the company that I built.”

The second-best way to make money in the stock market is to buy when others are panicking and liquidating their holdings at steep discounts. To capitalize on those periods, we must prepare in advance, reducing risk exposure by storing cash to support our financial and psychological wherewithal.

Because common shares have no maturity date, their duration—the time required to recover the investment—is effectively infinite. In practice, this means that equities behave like very long-duration assets, roughly comparable to 20- to 40-year securities, because most of their value derives from earnings far in the future. Growth stocks that pay little or no dividends therefore have the most extended duration, while dividend-paying equities have shorter duration. By contrast, government bonds, with fixed maturities and contractually defined interest payments, have much lower duration and repayment risk.

A financial business that enriches itself by selling risky securities to others has successfully convinced the masses to be price-indiscriminate buyers. Households are holding a record concentration of their net worth in equities.

At a dinner party last night, several attendees complained that their businesses were struggling even as stock markets were trading at all-time valuation highs. “It doesn’t make any sense,” bemoaned one head of the Americas for an international sports equipment and apparel manufacturer. An architect noted that major builders were calling him daily, hoping for leads for new projects as their existing pipeline dries up.

We cannot control others or world events; we can control our own behaviour, leverage, and risk exposure to market cycles within our finite lifespans. As our moms used to say, just because others are doing reckless things does not mean that we have to. There is power in autonomy, math and personal discipline.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Unfolding Fed Takeover: DOJ Vs. Powell
data.description:
Powell under criminal investigation

The U.S. attorney’s office in the District of Columbia has officially launched a criminal investigation against Fed Chair Powell, and this news was confirmed on Sunday, January 11th.

First, it's important to understand that this is only an investigation, and no official criminal charges have been filed against Powell yet.

Specifically, the DOJ has opened a criminal investigation into Powell’s testimony about the Federal Reserve headquarters renovation and whether Powell lied to Congress about the scope of the project. In addition, the DOJ has issued grand jury subpoenas to the Federal Reserve.

This means that the prosecutors will start collecting evidence against Powell and present this evidence to the grand jury, which will decide whether to file the official criminal charges.

The criminal charges against Powell can be filed under these statutes:

  • 18 U.S.C. § 1001 — making false statements to Congress
  • 18 U.S.C. § 1505 — obstructing a congressional inquiry

If the charges are filed under both of these statutes, the maximum sentence could be 0-5 years in prison and fines, although for a non-violent first-time offender, the likely sentence is probation.

Thus, even if found guilty, Powell would likely not go to prison.

Powell's response

Immediately after the breaking news about the criminal investigation, Powell responded via video statement.

Basically, Powell acknowledged that the criminal investigation is active and stated that the accusations are just "pretext". He argues that the investigation is politically motivated specifically due to his unwillingness to lower interest rates at the direction of President Trump.

For context, President Trump has been demanding lower interest rates from the Fed. One of the main issues here is the increasingly unsustainable interest payment on the US debt, and lower short-term interest rates are necessary to lower the interest payment - assuming that long-term interest rates also fall.

The Fed takeover

Powell's response suggests that President Trump has been trying to influence the Fed's monetary policy decisions, which are supposed to be independent from any influence and set primarily to accomplish the Fed's statutory mandate of 1) full employment and 2) price stability - for the public benefit.

The Fed sets the monetary policy via the Federal Open Market Committee ('FOMC'), which has 12 members, by a simple majority rule.

The FOMC consists of seven Fed Governors, appointed by the sitting President, and five Regional Fed Presidents, appointed by the respective regional Fed. The Fed Governors and the NY Fed President are the permanent voters, while four out of the 11 remaining regional Fed presidents rotate each year for the vote on the FOMC.

The current voting FOMC makeup is as follows:

  • The Fed Governors Bowman, Waller, and Miran are Doves, supporting Trump's view on interest rates - that's three votes.
  • The Fed Governors Powell, Jefferson, Barr, and Cook are Centrists, supporting the Fed's independence, or setting the interest rates based on the macro outlook independently of any influence - that's four votes.
  • Note, Powell is the FOMC Chair, whose term as the Chair expires in May, but his term as a Fed Governor extends until 2028.
  • The NY Fed Chair Williams is also Centrist, and a permanent FOMC voter - that adds to five centrist votes.
  • Thus, from the permanent FOMC voters, the tally is 5-3 for Centrists. President Trump has already filed charges against Governor Cook for mortgage fraud, and if found guilty, Cook will be fired, which will allow Trump to appoint another Dove to the FOMC and have a balanced 4-4 FOMC makeup.
  • However, to gain the 5-3 control, Trump needs Powell to resign as a Governor once his term as the Chair expires in May - so that's the key.

Thus, based on this analysis, the criminal investigation against Powell is the pretext for grounds to dismiss Powell as a Governor once his term as the Chair expires in May. But note, the Supreme Court also needs to find Cook guilty so she could be fired.

However, even if Trump gains a 5-3 advantage in permanent voters, there are still four votes outstanding from the regional Fed presidents. For 2026 these are the voting regional Fed presidents:

  • Philadelphia Fed President Paulson seems to be Dovish.
  • Minneapolis Fed President Kashkari seems to be Hawkish.
  • Dallas Fed President Logan and Cleveland Fed President Hammack are both Hawks.

Thus, the current FOMC make seems to be 8-4 for the Centrist-Hawkish group. If Powell and Cook are fired and replaced by the Doves, the balance would be 6-6 - and that could be a nightmare scenario for the financial markets.

In this situation, the Philadelphia Fed President Paulson would be the key swing vote.

The Neutral rate is the key point

The Doves led by Miran, are basically arguing that the neutral rate is much lower than the currently estimated 3% level.

The FOMC is currently implementing a policy normalization, or reduction in the Federal Funds rate from the restrictive to the neutral level, and the process is nearly completed, with two additional 25 bpt cuts remaining to the 3% neutral level.

The Doves are arguing that the policy normalization should continue until possibly the 2% level, as this is an appropriate Neutral rate.

The market implications

The Fed has been cutting interest rates outside a recession - that's called policy normalization.

There are two risks associated with policy normalization.

  • First, if the Fed is too slow, the labor market could continue to weaken and eventually cause a recession.
  • Second, if the policy normalization is too aggressive and exceeds the neutral rate to the accommodative stance outside a recession, the risk is high inflation.

Thus, if the FOMC takeover is successful, the Fed would likely lower interest rates well into the accommodative area and cause higher inflation. At that point, the Fed would be forced to increase interest rates. But if the FOMC is still influenced by the administration and refuses to increase rates even with rising inflation, the risk is an inflationary shock.

In this situation, the market risk is the US Dollar (DXY) collapse, sharply rising long term interest rates (US10Y) (TLT) due to de-anchoring of long-term inflation expectations, and, obviously, a stock market crash (SP500).

With the news of Powell's criminal investigation, Gold (GLD) jumped by over 2%, and Silver (SLV) spiked by over 7% - and the precious metals market has been the only asset class pricing the inflationary shock risk.

The initial reaction to the Powell news from the stock market has been muted; the US dollar only slightly weakened, while the bond market barely moved. The interpretation is that the market does not believe that the Fed takeover will be successful, which is logical reasoning.

In my opinion, the Supreme Court will rule against firing Fed Governor Cook, but Powell might resign as the Governor once his term as the Chair expires. Thus, the FOMC will remain independent, possibly by 1 vote, which is not very comforting.

The risk is that the administration continues to legally "pressure" other FOMC members until the Fed takeover is complete.

Investors should pay close attention to the 10Y Treasury Bond yield, and specifically the 10Y BE inflation expectations, and be concerned once the 10Y BE expectations cross the 2.5% level. Currently, we are at around 2.3%, thus breakaway inflation is currently not an issue, which means that the Powell news is not concerning for now.

data.title: Sandisk: The AI NAND Winner Wall Street Still Underestimates
data.description:
Sandisk Corporation (SNDK) has emerged as the best-performing memory stock, surging more than 800% in the past year following its spinoff into a separate entity from Western Digital Corporation (WDC) in February 2025. The stock has surged in tandem with underlying business fundamentals, benefitting from accelerating memory and storage demand to support the ongoing AI transformation.

Specifically, Sandisk is not only a key beneficiary of favorable growth dynamics that stem from AI-driven NAND demand, which currently include resilient pricing power propelled by a constrained supply environment, as well as rising bit content to facilitate increasingly complex devices. Instead, the company’s targeted innovations are actually also breaking traditional scalability roadblocks for NAND use cases in accelerated computing. This is expected to reinforce its long-term total addressable market and prospects in monetizing the accelerating buildout of critical AI infrastructure.

Looking ahead, the accelerating ramp up of Sandisk’s latest BiCS8 node remains a key growth driver, enabling meaningful capture of near-term pricing and bit growth tailwinds – especially in the supply-constrained memory market. At the same time, its emerging high-bandwidth flash (“HBF”) technology targeting data center applications also highlights additional growth headroom that remains underappreciated in current estimates priced into the stock. This accordingly underscores a favorable backdrop for further upside realization ahead.

Why Does Memory Matter in AI?

Unlike server CPUs that’ve been historically favored in general-purpose data centers, the currently preferred data center GPUs used in accelerated computing are much more powerful and relevant in facilitating increasingly complex AI workloads. The difference in performance stems from their differentiated processing. Specifically, general-purpose CPUs are known to process computing tasks in a “serial fashion” – or one task at a time. Meanwhile, data center GPUs complement as an accelerator to server CPUs by executing computing tasks in a “parallel fashion” – or multiple tasks across multiple processors at the same time. This has significantly reduced latency in compute, which has been critical in addressing demanding AI workloads spanning recommender systems to generative AI chatbots.

This is where the increasingly mission critical role of memory in the era of AI compute kicks in. In order for accelerators like Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs to process multiple tasks at the same time, they’ll inevitably need quick access to data. This has accordingly introduced the need for increased memory storage within the AI processor themselves, hence the advent of high-bandwidth memory, or “HBM,” momentum in recent years. Specifically, HBM is a DRAM solution – it’s “volatile memory” that loses the data when power is off; but when the power is on, it uses a capacitor and transistor cell to enable instant response, allowing data to be read and written faster. Although DRAM’s more expensive than NAND, the technology’s taken precedence in data center applications due to its comparative advantage in addressing speed and capacity needs in increasingly complex AI workloads.

Meanwhile, NAND is also increasingly used in AI data center applications. NAND is “non-volatile memory,” meaning it retains data even when power’s turned off. Given the increasing need to save large amounts of data output, NAND has also emerged as a critical memory storage solution in AI compute.

Why Is Sandisk Benefitting From AI?

Sandisk is currently a leading developer, manufacturer and provider of NAND solutions tailored for application across data center, edge and consumer use cases. The company’s emerged as a key beneficiary of recent pricing tailwinds in memory, complemented by structurally rising bit demand as content requirements expand to facilitate increasingly complex processing needs. This includes increasing memory content in edge devices like smartphones and PCs, which are currently positioned for an AI-driven upgrade cycle. There are also increasing NAND demand from autonomy and robotics use cases, which Sandisk management expects to expand at a multi-year CAGR of 12%.

This has propelled a persistent increase in both DRAM and NAND prices in recent years. Most recently, the average selling price of DRAM has increased by 40% sequentially during the December quarter, while NAND’s also steadily recovered with pricing growth at 20% over the same period. Industry leaders are also unanimously cautioning of a persistent pricing uptrend:

  • Dell (DELL): Dell’s recently warned about extended memory cost inflation, as both HBM and non-HBM memory demand continues to outpace supply availability. The company’s highlighted that 70% of its server installed base is pending upgrade to newer generation, AI-accommodating infrastructure. This accordingly highlights persistent supply constraints in DRAM and NAND availability, protracting memory’s pricing tailwind.
  • HP Enterprise (HPE): HPE warned during its fiscal Q4 earnings that there’ve already been signals across DRAM suppliers about imminent pricing actions due to supply constraints. Similar trends are expected to follow for NAND in 2026, reinforcing memory’s persistent pricing tailwind.
  • Samsung (SSNLF): Samsung’s more than tripled its earnings in the December quarter after benefiting from memory’s price uplift. The company, alongside its peer SK Hynix, is reportedly mulling a price increase of as much as 70% on server DRAM prices in the current period.

This is consistent with recent reports that Sandisk’s preparing to “double the price of its high-capacity 3D NAND memory devices for enterprise-grade solid-state drivers [SSDs]” in the current period. This pricing tailwind is likely to spill over into 3D NAND supply for its consumer and edge end-markets as well, reinforcing prospects for an impending uplift to current growth estimates priced into the Sandisk stock.

Overcoming Traditional Roadblocks in NAND-Based AI Applications

As mentioned in the earlier section, NAND has historically been the less preferred than its DRAM counterpart in the “memory hierarchy” for AI application due to its higher latency. However, recent innovations at Sandisk have essentially overcome these roadblocks:

BiCS8

BiCS8 is currently Sandisk’s leading NAND process node for data center application.

The BiCS8 node is currently used in several of Sandisk’s quad-level cell (“QLC”) designs that target enterprise SSD for data center application. QLCs entail 16 charge levels and 4 bits versus eight charge levels and three bits in triple-level cells (“TLC”). Although QLC is just one bit more than TLC NAND flash, it has double the number of voltage levels that the controller needs to distinguish. This typically means slower response times, given lengthened write speeds to ensure precise programming of data across all 16 charge levels, and increased read latency as the controller performs additional voltage sensing to identify the correct state and translate the charge back into bits for data processing.

Yet Sandisk’s BiCS8-based QLC NAND portfolio overcomes the traditional roadblocks in QLCs, making them relevant in terms of both efficiency and performance within demanding AI compute environments. Specifically, the BiCS8 2TB QLC die enables double storage density while maintaining compact die size. This is currently used in the 128TB NVMe SSD, which is currently undergoing qualifications across multiple hyperscaler customers, and expected to start ramping in the first half of calendar 2026. Meanwhile, a more powerful UltraQLC 256TB NVMe SSD configuration is also in the works, with go-to-market expected in the latter half of calendar 2026 and anticipation for a stronger ramp heading into calendar 2027.

Based on Sandisk’s fiscal Q1 earnings update, the BiCS8 node currently accounts for only 15% of total bits shipped despite “industry-leading capacity, I/O performance and energy efficiency.” This highlights significant headroom for further growth, especially in demanding data center applications, as the 128TB SSD ramps alongside the impending go-to-market for UltraQLC 256TB SSD. The emerging prospect is also in line with management’s expectations for the BiCS8 node to accelerate into the majority share of bit production exiting fiscal 2026, which is only about six months away.

This also corroborates management’s expectations for the data center end-market to grow into the largest NAND buyer in calendar 2026, besting the mobile end-market’s historical leadership. And Sandisk’s BiCS8 node makes it well-positioned to capitalize on the emerging secular trend.

High-Bandwidth Flash (“HBF”)

In addition to the recent ramp of its BiCS8 node, the emerging high-bandwidth flash (“HBF”) architecture could become a defining product for Sandisk’s foray in data center NAND application.

Although the product’s not expected to ship until at least 2027, which management has recently said the company remains on track to deliver, it could potentially represent a major growth inflection for Sandisk. Specifically, HBF is an “inference-based solution”, aligning with the emerging shift in AI compute dynamics where model training workloads will no longer dominate. The HBF solution currently under development will essentially give Sandisk and its partner SK hynix (HXSC.F) a first-mover advantage within an underserved corner of AI-memory requirement.

Based on a recent Sandisk blog post, HBF was invented to address the widening gap between compute and memory needs – especially in edge AI applications. Specifically, increasingly powerful AI foundation models are not only requiring significant compute power, but also memory at the same time. Yet the increase in compute performance does not align in tandem with LLMs’ memory demand, especially given “stagnant improvements in DRAM technology,” hence creating a “memory wall”:

To facilitate the surging memory demand from increasingly complex models, such as those with multi-modal capabilities, data centers have tried bridge the gap by installing larger GPU clusters. Yet the same strategy can’t be applied in edge AI applications, given limited space. And this is where the relevance of HBF comes in.

While HBM is a DRAM solution, as discussed earlier, HBF is a NAND alternative. The NAND-based HBF architecture offers “8 to 16x the capacity of HBM,” without compromising read bandwidth and TCO. This essentially enables terabytes of additional memory to GPUs within the data center, as HBF augments HBM. Meanwhile, HBF alone can help unlock processing for complex and intensive AI models on edge devices, as it addresses “cost, power and/or space limitations.”

HBF is also expected to gain relevance in AI compute, as developers focus on reducing compute requirement while the need for memory capacity increases. This is known as the “memory-centric AI paradigm,” and aligns with the increasing use of “architectural innovations” in LLM development, such as Mixture of Experts (“MoE”), to reduce compute requirements, while models become more powerful and memory-intensive. For instance, many of DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) models have utilized the MoE architecture to unlock significant compute efficiencies amid China’s curtailed access to the latest and greatest Nvidia AI processors, while their growing complexity, such as multi-modal capabilities, is simultaneously increasing memory demand.

More importantly, early case studies have shown comparable performance between HBF and HBM in handling state-of-the-art LLMs currently available in the market, providing validity to the viability and relevance of Sandisk’s impending innovation. A recent simulation model that compared HBF-based GPU processing and HBM-based GPU processing on a 400-billion-parameter Llama 3.1 model (META) showed comparable performance within a 2% variance across “various stages of the inference engine flow”:

Sandisk’s Advantage Lies in Innovating NAND Scalability

Innovation aside, fundamental success relies significantly on scalability – and that’s exactly what Sandisk’s achieved, which is evident in its accelerating margin expansion.

Specifically, lateral, logical and vertical scaling have long dominated the broader semiconductor sector.

Lateral scaling refers to the optimized use of space within a specific chip. In logic processors, we see lateral scaling in effect from improving process nodes that aim to optimize the number of transistors within a fixed physical chip measurement. For instance, industry leader TSMC’s (TSM) latest N2 manufacturing process effectively reduces transistor distance to 2 nanometers. This has been a critical enabler of Nvidia’s high-performing GPUs and its capability in scaling increasingly power-intensive AI workloads. And in memory, the goal is to optimize cell placement to maximize the amount of bits that can be “packed in every square millimeter of every memory layer.”

In logical scaling, the goal is to optimize the “number of bits of data that can be stored in a single NAND cell.” Instead of scaling “across,” logical scaling is essentially scaling “within.” As mentioned earlier, QLC and TLC are technologies that’ve enabled multi-bit storage within each cell by relying on different levels of charge. While architectures like QLC increase storage capacity and lowers TCO, they could also introduce higher latency and reduced reliability due to the increased processes and, inadvertently, risks of error in reading and writing data. Sandisk’s overcome this challenge by optimizing system-level improvements on a use-case basis, enabling efficient and simultaneous lateral and logical scaling across its NAND solutions. This is the technology that underpins the performance of its 128TB enterprise SSD and upcoming UltraQLC 256TB SSD, as well as their scalability to match industry token spend optimization needs.

Sandisk has furthered scalability of its technology through vertical scaling, enabled by the shift to 3D NAND architectures. This has addressed previous physical roadblocks in laterally scaling traditional 2D NAND, which relied on shrinking the components to pack more within a chip. 3D NAND allows vertical scaling by essentially “stacking layers of semiconductor material on top of each other,” increasing density and memory performance while staying within limitations of a chip’s physical footprint. And Sandisk, alongside its partner Kioxia, have been critical pioneers in 3D NAND flash memory technology. Specifically, their recently introduced the 9th generation 3D flash memory, which is optimized for inference by enhancing “power efficiency of data input/output”, increasing interface speed and reducing power consumption.

More importantly, Sandisk’s key competitive advantage comes from its innovation in architectural scaling, enabled by its joint-development of the BiCS8 node with Kioxia. Specifically, the BiCS8 node’s increasing relevance for NAND integration in AI use cases is made possible by Sandisk-Kioxia’s (KXIAY) innovation in “CMOS directly Bonded to Array” – or “CBA.” This is a breakthrough memory architecture that further addresses existing scaling roadblocks face by NAND flash in demanding data center use cases. Historically, CMOS – which is the logic processor that controls memory cells – is bonded with the NAND array on the same wafer. The trade-off in this legacy architecture’s design comes from the CMOS and NAND flash’s “different thermal and processing parameters during their construction,” which essentially hinders both from reaching their respective optimized performance:

When CMOS is built under the array (“CUA”), the subsequent high-temperature NAND processing steps impose a “thermal budget” limitation on the CMOS, preventing its full optimization.

Source: NAND Scaling Explained, Sandisk Blog.

Yet the innovative CBA architecture addresses this limitation. CBA allows each of the NAND array and CMOS to be built on separate wafers, then subsequently “bonded” back together. This essentially allows both to be optimized, furthering memory scalability in demanding use cases. The CBA technology is what currently enables Sandisk’s newest BiCS8 2TB QLC die, which is powers the 128TB SSD and next-generation UltraQLC 256TB SSD optimized for data center and edge AI applications.

Fundamental Considerations

Taken together, it’s evident that Sandisk’s emerging NAND roadmap and focus on enabling scaling efficiencies across memory needs in the burgeoning AI opportunity represents an extended growth tailwind for the company. This is corroborated by management’s expectation for data center applications to represent the largest market for NAND in calendar 2026, outpacing historical strength driven by mobile applications.

I expect the company to expand its revenue at a four-year CAGR of 13% through FY 2030, which compares with the current consensus estimate at a 4% CAGR over the same period. My projection is primarily driven by expectations of data center revenue growth at a 42% CAGR through FY 2030. This would align closer with the pace of accelerating bit demand that’s now reached the mid-20% range without accounting for incremental pricing tailwinds driven by protracted supply constraints that are expected to last through at least 2027. It’s also corroborated by current management expectations for data center memory storage demand to exceed 40% growth towards “high 300s” exabytes through 2026, with further acceleration through 2027. My base case growth projection also aligns with Sandisk’s emerging HBF opportunity as edge memory demand expands with rising AI adoption – a major tailwind that likely remains underappreciated.

Continued acceleration in Sandisk’s data center operations is further corroborated by shifting contract dynamics currently observed by management, where “really big players” – likely hyperscalers – have been increasingly reaching out to secure multi-year supplies. This could potentially increase Sandisk’s mix of long-term “volume price commitments” that exceed a one-year horizon, which has historically been low and kept within a quarter’s time. The shift could reinforce the company’s visibility into long-term pricing, and mitigate its exposure to the memory industry’s inherent sensitivity to cyclical headwinds.

So we don't have volume -- we have very little volume price commitments that are beyond a quarter. So that's -- what we referred to in our prepared remarks is what we are hearing now from some of our very strategic customers, very large customers is they want assurance on supply. So they have approached us willing to start some of those conversations to see if there is a volume price commitment that we can agree for the year, potentially for longer.

Source: Sandisk Q1 FY 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

Accelerating data center revenue is expected to further BiCS8’s ramp trajectory, which is consistent with management’s expectations for the node to drive the majority of Sandisk’s bit production mix exiting FY 2026. This is expected to unlock further margin expansion at scale for Sandisk, given the new-generation node’s cost tailwinds.

Specifically, the strong BiCS8 ramp-up will reduce Sandisk’s historical exposure to underutilization and fab start-up expenses, which represented a cost headwind of 320 basis points in F1Q (i.e., non-GAAP gross margin 29.9% vs. 33.1% excluding $11 million underutilization and $61 million start-up costs). Looking ahead, these costs are expected to approach zero. This is consistent with management’s guidance for non-GAAP gross margin of 41% to 43% in the current period, which represents 1,210 bps sequential expansion at the midpoint, accelerating from the 350-bps improvement observed in F1Q26 from F4Q25. This is consistent with expectations for start-up costs to drop from $61 million in F1Q to $30 million in the current period, with further improvements toward zero going forward. Meanwhile, underutilization costs are also now behind Sandisk, with its fabs now operating at 100% capacity to address accelerating end-market demand.

Price Considerations

Sandisk currently trades at 4.2x FY 2027 revenue based on the average consensus estimate of 25% y/y growth for the period. This underperforms the average of 4.7x FY 2027 revenue currently observed across its comparable peers, while the cohort’s mean growth over the same period’s only 18%. On a proportional basis alone, Sandisk should trade closer to 6.4x FY 2027 revenue, which represents 53% upside from current levels. This is supported with my earlier discussed expectation that Sandisk could be well positioned for an incremental growth uplift, as its HBF tailwind likely remains underappreciated in current estimates priced in.

From an earnings perspective, Sandisk is currently trading at 17.6x FY 2027 earnings based on the average consensus estimate for 62% y/y EPS growth over the same period. This barely outperforms the current average of 16.7x FY 2027 earnings observed across its comparable peers, which are only expected to deliver mean EPS growth of 36% over the same period. On a proportional basis, Sandisk should trade closer to 28.8x FY 2027 earnings, which represents upside of at least 64% from current levels. At 28.8x FY 2027 earnings, Sandisk would be trading much more adequately compared to the average 29x forward P/E observed across key AI enablers like the Magnificent Seven. This accordingly corroborates further upside potential, especially given significant earnings accretion stemming from Sandisk’s scale advantage and the continued ramp of its BiCS8 bit production mix.

Taken together, I expect a base case price target of $599 per share – a weighed outcome of the above multiple-based considerations.

In the upside scenario, Sandisk’s growth and earnings profile is likely to exceed the current consensus estimate and approach closer to my projections. This would be consistent with current NAND demand dynamics, with resilience against industry’s inherent sensitivity to cyclical headwinds preserved by the increasing mix of fixed long-term volume pricing contracts and secular AI memory requirements. By applying the same methodology used in computing the base case price to my upside scenario estimates, Sandisk’s price could reach $617 per share.

Downside Risks to Consider

Despite Sandisk’s strong positioning in the AI-driven “memory supercycle,” it remains prone to industry’s inherent cyclical nature. Although current supply constraints reinforce a pricing tailwind, and long-term volume pricing contracts could improve fundamental visibility, any cyclical weakness in the economy could pressure Sandisk’s performance.

This is corroborated by recent data that points to a weakening labor market, which could potentially signal an impending slowdown. However, this risk is likely to be offset by Sandisk’s conservative approach in adding wafer capacity. This also highlights the advantage of Sandisk’s architectural scaling discussed in the earlier section, which enables the company to “increase productivity and bit supply without increasing wafers.”

So first of all, on the overall environment, I think we've been talking for, I don't know, at least a year now that we saw an undersupplied market through the end of '26, and that's mainly focused on looking at long-term demand trends and then what capital has been invested in this business over the last several years and factoring in nodal transitions of all the players in the market…As far as what does it take, we're not at the phase of talking about additional capital in this business. We're investing a significant amount of capital, as Luis said, to do the BiCS8 transition. We have a very, very strong technology road map where we can increase productivity and bit supply without increasing wafers.

Source: Sandisk Q1 FY 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

Final Thoughts

It’s evident that Sandisk’s emerged as a structural beneficiary of the ongoing AI transformation, with its roadmap adequately positioned to capture emerging shifts in compute requirements. Unlike prior memory cycles, which have largely coincided with economic and spending strength, Sandisk’s current demand environment is backed by a secular shift in compute architectures to accommodate the transition to increasingly complex AI workloads.

More importantly, it’s emerging NAND roadmap’s focus on scalability also aligns well with the impending industry shift to inference and token spend optimization. This is expected to further Sandisk’s reach into burgeoning data center opportunities, unlocking incremental upside to its earnings profile at scale. Taken together, Sandisk remains well positioned for further valuation accretion, as its NAND portfolio takes on an increasingly critical role in evolving AI compute dynamics.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2026 - The Year Of Palantir
data.description:
Palantir's (PLTR) stock has been consolidating for the past few months, even as the firm's results surpassed management's and analyst's forecasts.

If we look underneath the hood, this is a healthy development, particularly as Palantir's fundamentals continue to improve. The rule of 40 - a sum of revenue growth and operating margins - is now at 114%, which marks an all-time high.

In Q3 2025, the commercial sales grew 121% YoY, up from 92% reported in the previous quarter and the profitability stayed very high.

The firm's performance is a clear example of American exceptionalism, and 2026 isn't going to bring a reversal to this trend.

What we are observing however, is Palantir's valuation acting as an anchor on the stock's performance. Palantir's rapid growth trajectory is set to continue, while it remains an AI leader, but the valuation barrier may stay with us for the next few months, until earnings don't catch up to the outsized expectations.

With a P/E of 244x, Palantir's valuation will contract in the next months/years as EPS will grow 30-40% annually, nevertheless it remains the most expensive stock in our coverage and the margin of error is very slim.

Previous Coverage

In my last coverage titled: How Large Can Palantir Grow, we discussed Palantir's business model in-depth, the valuation, and it's path into the future.

Back in September, government-focused business still made up 55% of the firm's revenue, while commercial remained at 45%. However, I've argued that it won't take too long for the commercial business to become the main revenue driver thanks to the 90-100% revenue YoY growth rates.

The commercial AIP platform is easily scalable and easy to configure and integrate using customer's data in combination with LLM's while meeting all the corporate-required security standards. But, the best part is, the deployment won't take more than a few days for AIP.

This is the main reason why I believe Palantir is on a path to become $1T market cap firm - a 2.5x growth from the back-then $400B valuation.

I've argued that this is a major leap in revenue, profit margins and scale of business and without question, successful execution.

Based on a 40% annual revenue growth rate, we've concluded at if the stock would be priced at P/S of 50, it would take Palantir 5.13 years to reach this milestone with the firm's revenue at $20B/year.

Valuation & Risk

Today we start a bit of unconventionally - with the valuation as this is the center piece of Palantir's conversation.

In finance, the term overvalued/expensive is tossed around all the time. For value-oriented investors a firm with P/E 20+ would be expensive. For some a P/E of 40x is completely acceptable.

But it isn't about the P/E. Valuation has to be gauged against the forward-looking growth expectations. For instance, Nvidia (NVDA) is priced at 40x P/E, but it's EPS is projected to grow by 56% in FY27 - would you consider this overvalued/expensive? I wouldn't. The risk is the forecast may not materialize which would change the thesis.

Palantir is currently priced at a P/E of 244x, or 120x it's revenue. This is a 350-400% premium over other AI companies. It's a very steep valuation. In this instance, even if we gauge it against the forward EPS growth, which according to FactSet is as follows:

  • FY25 EPS: $0.72E, YoY growth: 76%
  • FY26 EPS: $0.99E, YoY growth: 38%
  • FY27 EPS: $1.35E, YoY growth: 36%

These projections increased meaningfully since summer. E.g. back in July full-year 2025 EPS was expected to land at $0.58, or 41% growth. Today it's $0.72, or 76% growth, yet P/E hasn't expanded any further.

Altogether, I see 38% EPS growth in FY26 and 36% in FY27 as low estimates, likely to be revised later and be in the range of 45-60%.

Now, a P/E of 244x with a 76% growth or 30-40% in the subsequent years is too low for my liking and it carries far too much risk for my portfolio. But, the narrative here is a bit different compared to the one of Nvidia.

Palantir's size is far smaller. The firms' annual revenue run-rate is $4B and it's trailing-twelve-month operating income sits below $1B. To put it into perspective, Nvidia's revenue is ≈$190B and operating income is $110B. If you compare these two, you see that Palantir's growth runway will be much longer, while the firm should be able to maintain 30-40% annual growth rate for years to come.

Would I say the risk/reward is right with Palantir right now?

No, I wouldn't. Speaking from experience, if we look back to the 1970s, such steep valuation is very rare to justify and history points to valuation compression. Palantir can continue defying the odds of gravity and the market, and it could be the one outlier, yet it's growth in my view should expand to 40-50% EPS growth to justify the current valuation and in fact earn a return to investor's buying the shares today, ≈$180/share.

For me to take a stab at the shares, I would need to see 80-120x P/E valuation with the same growth profile. That would be a price of $80-$90/share.

Even at that price, to keep risk under control I wouldn't commit more than 1.5-2% of my portfolio here.

If I were sitting on a 1,000% gain, as many SA readers are, I would just keep holding the stock and look elsewhere with a new capital.

The upside is still there, but valuation may weigh on the performance over the foreseeable future.

Business Update

The power of Palantir's commercial business lies in the way it helps to shape decision-making, by aggregating and cleaning data in the most complex datasets.
